intro:
Mulch is one of the most beneficial things you can add to your garden beds — it retains soil moisture, suppresses weeds, regulates soil temperature, improves soil health as it decomposes, and enhances curb appeal. But ordering the right amount requires knowing your area and desired depth.

Our mulch calculator converts your garden bed area and desired mulch depth into cubic yards (for bulk delivery) and cubic feet, plus how many 2-cubic-foot bags you would need from a garden center. The recommended depth for mulch is 2–3 inches for existing beds and 4 inches for new beds or heavy weed suppression.

Mulch compacts and decomposes over time. Most garden beds need refreshing annually (adding 1–2 inches). Over-mulching (more than 4 inches) can actually harm plants by retaining too much moisture and creating anaerobic conditions around roots.
howItWorks: Enter the total area of your garden beds in square feet (length × width for each bed, add them together) and desired mulch depth in inches. The calculator converts depth to feet, multiplies by area for cubic feet, divides by 27 for cubic yards, and divides cubic feet by 2 to show bags of 2-cubic-foot mulch needed.
interpretationGuide: Cubic yards is the unit for bulk mulch delivery (minimum 1 yard from most suppliers). Cubic feet helps plan bag quantities. Bags are economical for small projects; bulk delivery is dramatically cheaper per cubic yard for larger jobs. Add 10% buffer for uneven beds and edges.

faqs.question: How deep should mulch be?
faqs.answer: 2–3 inches for established garden beds and around trees. 3–4 inches for new beds or heavy weed suppression. Never exceed 4 inches total — deep mulch retains too much moisture and can rot plant crowns. Keep mulch 2–3 inches away from tree trunks and plant stems ('volcano mulching' harms trees).

faqs.question: How many cubic yards of mulch do I need for my garden?
faqs.answer: Measure total garden bed square footage, then use this formula: Cubic yards = (Area × Depth in inches) ÷ 324. For 500 sq ft at 3 inches: (500 × 3) ÷ 324 = 4.63 cubic yards, so order 5 yards.

faqs.question: What is better: bagged mulch or bulk delivery?
faqs.answer: Bulk delivery is dramatically cheaper for any project over 2–3 cubic yards. A 2-cu-ft bag at $4–$7 equals $54–$95 per cubic yard. Bulk mulch costs $25–$45 per cubic yard delivered. The break-even point is roughly 50–75 bags — above that, bulk always wins on cost.

faqs.question: What is the best type of mulch?
faqs.answer: Shredded hardwood/bark: most common, decomposes slowly, enriches soil. Cedar: natural insect repellent, slow to decompose. Pine bark nuggets: attractive, stays in place well. Straw: cheap, great for vegetable gardens. Rubber mulch: long-lasting, good for playgrounds, does not enrich soil. Cocoa mulch: fragrant but toxic to dogs.

faqs.question: How much does mulch cost?
faqs.answer: Bagged (2 cu ft): $4–$7 per bag. Bulk delivery: $25–$65 per cubic yard including delivery. Premium colored mulch costs more than natural. One pickup truck load = approximately 1 cubic yard (stacked level). Some landscaping companies include mulch installation in their price.

faqs.question: How do I calculate my total garden bed area?
faqs.answer: For rectangular beds, multiply length × width for each bed. Estimate circular beds with π × r². For irregular shapes, break them into rough rectangles and add. Take your time getting accurate measurements — even rough estimates within 10% are fine for mulch ordering.

faqs.question: Does mulch attract pests?
faqs.answer: Properly maintained mulch does not generally attract pests. Termites can be a concern with wood mulch placed directly against house foundations — maintain a 12-inch clear zone between mulch and the foundation. Slugs and snails may proliferate in very thick, moist mulch. Cedar mulch has natural repellent properties.

faqs.question: When is the best time to mulch?
faqs.answer: Spring: after the soil has warmed (mulching too early keeps cold soil cold longer). Fall: after plants go dormant to insulate soil over winter. Avoid mulching in late summer when plants are still active — it can trap moisture and promote fungal issues. Any season works for ornamental beds.

faqs.question: Should I remove old mulch before adding new?
faqs.answer: Generally, no. Old mulch is decomposing and enriching your soil — raking it away removes that benefit. Simply rake existing mulch to loosen it (prevents matting), check that depth has not exceeded 4 inches, and add new mulch on top to refresh the layer. Remove only if old mulch is diseased or heavily compacted.

faqs.question: How do I prevent mulch from washing away?
faqs.answer: Install edging (metal, plastic, or stone borders) to contain mulch. Use finer mulch materials (shredded bark) that interlock instead of rolling nuggets. Avoid steep slopes without retaining edging. For sloped areas, use erosion control blankets beneath mulch or switch to heavier river rock that resists washout.
